title: Dog Yellow Crusty Eye Discharge: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: Noticing yellow crust around your dog's eyes? Learn what causes it, the urgency level, and why a photo can help your vet provide better care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Yellow crusty eye discharge usually indicates a mild infection or irritation and is typically not an emergency unless accompanied by squinting, pain, or cloudiness.

What does it mean if my dog has yellow crusty eye discharge?

Yellow or greenish discharge, often referred to as 'sleep' or 'eye gunk,' is typically a sign that your dog's immune system is reacting to something. While clear discharge is often just a result of allergies or dust, yellow crust usually suggests the presence of white blood cells. This is frequently seen with conditions like bacterial conjunctivitis, dry eye (KCS), or mild infections triggered by environmental irritants.

When is yellow crusty eye discharge in dogs considered an emergency?

The urgency level for yellow crusty discharge is generally Low, but you should determine your next steps based on these clinical signs:

  • Low Urgency: If your dog is acting normally, eating well, and not in obvious pain, this can usually wait for a scheduled appointment.
  • High Urgency: If you notice squinting, extreme redness, or if the eye itself looks cloudy, seek veterinary care immediately as these can be signs of a painful corneal ulcer.

Why should I provide a photo of my dog's eye for veterinary triage?

Sharing a clear, well-lit photo of your dog’s eyes with your veterinarian can significantly speed up the triage process by allowing a professional to:

  • Assess the exact shade and consistency of the discharge.
  • Evaluate the degree of inflammation in the surrounding tissue.
  • Determine if the eye surface appears intact.
  • Decide if your pet needs an urgent fit-in appointment or if the visit can wait a few days.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, yellow, crusty eye discharge (mucopurulent) can stem from several causes including generalized blepharitis from dermatophytes, Demodex canis, staphylococci, or immune-mediated diseases like pemphigus. It can also be a sign of inflammation and prolapse of the gland of the nictitating membrane ("cherry eye"), common in young dogs, where a mucopurulent discharge is present alongside a swollen, protruding red glandular mass. Additionally, obstructions in the nasolacrimal duct can manifest as discharge. Care includes frequent cleansing and topical antibiotics; in cases of nasolacrimal duct obstruction, maintaining duct patency via temporary catheterization may be necessary. In irreversible damage to the nasolacrimal apparatus, surgical intervention to create a new drainage pathway may be considered.

Chapter: Ophthalmology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 491)

faqs.question: Can I use warm water to clean my dog's eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can gently use a soft cloth dampened with warm water to wipe away the crust. Always wipe away from the eye to avoid further irritation.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my dog?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human over-the-counter eye drops on a dog unless specifically directed by your veterinarian, as some ingredients can be harmful.

faqs.question: What if the discharge is only in one eye?
faqs.answer: Discharge in only one eye often indicates a localized issue like a scratch or a foreign object, whereas both eyes usually point toward allergies or a systemic infection.
